speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl Olson discusses his thrashing operation in the Ridge Country in the 1920s. Skills, crops, small farms and pleasures. He also tells of work in small sawmills, difficulties selling cars and running a service station in the Depression, the hard life of early homesteaders, Victor Anderson's humor, the need for regulation of society, prohibition stories and about the Bank of Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a friendly guy?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know, he would throw it away. But he wouldn't throw it away, he put it in his pack, shacked it up and back.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Then he come over to one place. He had a gun, 45 -70, you know, it was a pretty big gun.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And he looked and he said, 22, he said, you got a small 45 -70, haven't you? Yeah, he was quite a character, all right. Mills and Bormacreet, they all talked about him, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, Carl, I wanted to ask you about the threshing, because you never really told me about the operation that you had on the Ridge, the harvest operation. And you never really told me how you got going at it and what it was like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On doing what?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: On the harvest operation on the Ridge. Well, I got a job from the Hawkinsons, you know, I worked for him for three or four years. He said, I want you to come out and fire my engine for me, my father. And the harvest, he said, and I gave you $2 a day. So that was it. I thought that was great, you know, I make $2 a day, I was only about 15 years old.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I went with him and by the time I was over, he said, you had steam every morning, didn't you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yes, I said, well, he said, I'll give you two and a half, like the pitchers did. Then he hired me again and again, you know, I worked for him for about three or four years. Then he wanted to sell the rig to my brother, and we bought him out. And then we run that for about 10 years. We trashed all of Little Bear and up on Dry Creek and all over the country. We cleaned up the dairy, we trashed for 101 farmers one year, you know, that's something. Now you only trash for one. The barns also big, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could you do 101 farmers in one season?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It was out 54 days. And then we had the beans left, so we had to go back and trash them. And that took us 11 days, so we had 65 days in. And it was the first of November when we got through. It was a rainy fall, see. And it was out of trash range around 2, you know, and we got such a long run because we had the mud hooks on our engine. Mud hooks looked like this and about 6 inches long. And they were about that far apart, you know, as we would go. And that's how we got around and got all that trashing. And we trashed between the showers. In those days, you know, Little Bear, we had the trash most of the time in shocks. You took it right from the field and trashed it. And then the smaller farmers stacked all their grains, see, so they were in stacks. And that's why we keep it going. If there had been any wheat out there, it would have all rotten, you know, because it rained off and on, off and on, off and on, see. And we kept it going that way. I didn't think we'd ever trash the beans, but it happened to get nice day in the first November. It lasted about two weeks and we finished up on the 11th and going home and started snowing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think that, what year about would that have been? About what year would that have been? What year?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: About 1920, I think. I'm pretty sure I'm right on that. It could be 21 or 20, but it's either one of them days because I was in the Army in 1919, see, so I missed that harvest. And then I come back and we trashed one year and the next year, it could have been 21 and was wet. But the first year I come out from the Army, we had the big. We made a lot of money that year. Maybe that was the big year, I guess so. And it could have been 1921.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weren't there other thrashing crews around at the same time?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, but they couldn't move. They couldn't move in the machinery because they didn't have no mud hooks. And therefore, I told you about that big plank we had. I'll show you how it works. And I figured that out when I was working on the road, you know, with pulling the grater. And here we got stuck.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Here's the engine now. See, we stuck a 3x12 under the wheel here. It was 16 foot long, see. And then we wrapped the chain around here and around here. And then we stiff -locked it, they call it. There was some differential gear in it, you know, it was like a car has. But we put that lock in the pull. Well, then the engine would climb up that thing. See, it'd climb up the plank. And then we were out. That's the way we got out, you know, on the fields. Because once in a while the wheels would be soft and you would sink right down. Over here on this side of the dairy, we got on a flat field and we didn't stick that plank under. And it went down four feet before it stopped. But then it hit solid ground there and then come right out. I thought, here we are now. We were stuck for good. Come right out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what did you put the plank underneath? What was that you put underneath the engine?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Under the bullwheel. Under the wheel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See the big bullwheel here?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And this is folks. Then we put the plank under here. We dug a little bit and the plank stood like this. Then we tried the chain here and the chain come down and we tried it around the bullwheel. So it wrapped around the bullwheel. It was the same as a drum, you know. It had to come out. I see. So it pulled right up the chain, pulled it right up. That was over on the Grandin place here where it went down so deep. But the way I found it out, you know, I was, we hadn't had the rig done more than about a year. And we was grading the road. And we was following pretty close to the edge, you know. And we come to a place where they blowed out a stump and they'd fill that in with dirt, loose dirt, you know. It looked like the road. And it was wet then too, you know. When we got there the old bullwheel was dropped down and that hole in there was set with the grader, road grader behind it. So the boss said the road boss, he had the same name, I had two car holes. And now go home, we said, and get my team. And we'll get out of here some way, he said. There was no way. That wheel was just spinning, you know, in there. But it happened to be the telephone pole there. And I thought to myself, by God, I'll try this. I took the telephone pole and rammed it under the wheel, tied the chain on the stick lock, and out it went just like that. And there I was sitting waiting. He was going about an hour, you know. He couldn't figure out how I got out of that hole, you know. He just couldn't figure. So I showed him how I got out there. And that's how I happened to get wise to that team, you know. And that come out pretty handy in the harvest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well this year, when did it start raining that fall?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, it started about the last of August.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much threshing did you get in before that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: About 20 days, 15, 20 days is all. We got through in a little bear and we got up to Arnold's place. And I forgot who lives on it now. It's an old dam on it, it's about half way down the ridge. And I said to the crew, I thought it was going to rain. And the clouds were coming up and we were on the last shocking open field. We had 100 acres of oats or something there. So I said, we got to finish this now. It's going to get rain and this is the last shock we got. And you know, when the rain on the shock, you had to wait three, four days, and maybe more before it dried out. But it was stacked the rest of us. We finished up, but we had to run an hour overtime to get it through. And then the next, all the other threshing was stacked after that. So Arnold's workplace was stacked and Kelly's place and the place you were on, all of them. See, even when we went straight ahead, an old run took all the new runs, then over to Dry Creek, and then from Dry Creek up to Derry. That's how we got in such a long run. It was 54 days of rain and 11 days of beans, see. 101 farmers to trash for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did some farmers stack it instead of shocking it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, they had to pay to bring it together. They had to make bundle teams, you know, to keep the machine going, and they had to go out in the field and haul it to the machine. But when they stacked it, you put the machine in between there, and I had the pitcher with me, four of them, or five we had, and they pitched it in and then we moved to another setting. So long as it didn't rain, it was trash because the heads were dry. And the outside ones were a little wet. If they were too wet, we had to sometimes wait a day for that even, to let them dry off a little bit. We got to one place where they stacked the grains of four, you know. They had to know how to stack grain. That was an art then, too. The bundles had to lean out this way, so when the rain hit them, it'll run off. It's better that he got his bundle this way, and the rain would run into the stack for him. They can't stack it, he said. He had to catch the stack, you see. And we couldn't knock that grain out, and he run around there like he was crazy, that farmer. He said, we can't trash or nothing. I said, we can't do nothing with it. It was a little club, too, and that was the hardest week to knock out there ever was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The what? Little club weeks?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah. His son lives on the ridge there. Nilsen, even far from where you live. Bill Nilsen. His dad, you know, he was running around there, and I said, alright, if you don't want to trash your equipment, I'll throw the belt off. No, he said, you can't. And we finished him up, too, you know. And then when we come back, they follow the trash beans, and we trashed all around him, you know. I just don't have to tease him, you know. We used to go in the trash room for him. I wouldn't leave nobody, you know. Well, if I liked him or not. And he called up, and we was at Ryerson. He went in the trash, my beans is, well, I don't know, I said, I don't do any better job on beans than on wheat.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, he said, you got to come and trash and go. I figured I'd trash them all the time, but I just left him to the last guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you decide what order you were going to trash the farms in? How did you decide what order you were going to trash the farms in? How did you decide who was going to get it first?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it come out pretty good, so we didn't have much problem there. The lower end, the little bear ripened up faster than the woods did, you see. And we had to start down there somewhere so the water was ready. See, there was so many kinds of grain. We had fall wheat, and the fall wheat come in two, three weeks ahead of the spring grain. So we trashed the fall wheat, and then sometimes we had to go back and trash the oats for him afterwards. It wasn't ready. And that's the way we worked it, you know. And then we bought it by the farmers, and we didn't have the cook wagon or nothing. But I was used to trashing. I could hardly ever miss, you know. We'd be at your place for dinner and so on. I'd tell them in times when we could have them food ready. But it happened when we trashed beans once. It was hard to judge that. I told that next farmer that we'll be at your place for dinner. And by golly, you know, before I knew it, we was through with that guy. I took my car and run down there and told him that we'll be here for dinner. We wouldn't be there for dinner. That's the way it was. But the old lady got excited. She jumped in the bed and squealed and hollered. And the old man said, don't pay that head to the dad. He does those things quite often, he said. It was all bogan camp. He lived way down at the end of the ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, she expected you for dinner and you weren't going to be there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You were ready to move. Yeah, because she didn't have dinner ready. But I hardly ever missed on that. I was used to it. I knew how much feel everybody had, and I knew the braggers. You know, some of them brags, you got so much and all that, so you couldn't fool me there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they said they had more to thrash than they had? The yield was so good, huh? What was the food like in the farms? Did they cook real good for you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It was just like going to a picnic every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One feller asked me once, he said, what would you do if you had the chance to do something?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I'd be a trash man if I couldn't have sent us. Best job I ever had. It didn't last too long, I said, so we didn't get tired of it. And the good eats we got all the time, you know, and everything. And we slept in the straw stack. I said we were all together all the time. We had a good time and everything. On Saturday nights, we'd all go in the trap wagon. They called that a wagon with us, you know, to carry the forks and other stuff in the beds and everything. And we all drove home in that on Saturday nights. Then we'd come back on Sunday, Sunday evening.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, it was quite pretty good. I liked it because you got to know people and everything else, you know. And we wasn't out just to make money only, you know. That's one thing that we were well liked, you know, they wanted us if they could get us. Because we'd just think I would, I pulled one, for a whole mile to trash nine sacks of wheat.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We lost money in that. I didn't charge you any extra, either. And the smallest bean trashing ever was three sacks. I didn't like that very good. We already quit, you know, it was true. And he come over, he only lived about a mile from us. He wanted his beans trashed. God, you know, we had to fill up the boiler with water and steam up and pool over there and everything. So I didn't like it. Oh, he kept on, I thought I'd better go anyway. He's a neighbor. And when we got up to his barn up there, there was a bunch of boards about that high from the ground laid over. And he run and told me where to go.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Where in hell is the bean stack at?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I couldn't think that. So I got off and I looked and I seen the beans on the...

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And he got three sacks.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: There was a place right next to the cemetery out there that happened. We lived down there where Emily is, you know, you can see the place from the cemetery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you work out the charge? How much it would cost them to thrash?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, we had so much bushel, so much sack. We got eight cents a sack for oats and ten cents for wheat. Ten cents a sack. See, they sacked all the grain and then the sack sowed for eighty -two and sold them up and piled them up, you know. But that was pretty reasonable. Most of them had two cents more than we did all the time. And then when we went out to the sea country, then they had the coquagons with them. Then they had about twenty cents a sack, I guess. But up in the woods there was so many small jobs that you couldn't afford it. And then another thing, you know, that book you give me, you could see there, I wondered if those fellas was on the small farm, there wouldn't be anything to trash. The horses would eat up everything. They even worried about that a lot of places a long time ago up here, you know. They had too many horses to come in there, they'd eat up all them grain. Well, a man that hundreds of sacks of wheat, that was a big job in those days. See, and then they had the oats too, you know. But that's the way it was. Small fields, fifteen, sixty acres had cleared up in the woods up here, you see. It was timber all over, you know, they had to get their fields wherever they could. And there was quite a job to clear off the land, you know. If it wasn't big timber, it was small black pine, they called it, get about this big, just as thick as they could grow. It's like sticks. So they didn't have very big fields, you know. Now, the little bear was pretty good because that was a ridge. And they claimed the Indians had fires on those ridges, grass fires, and they kept the small stuff down, but it was big yellow pines here and there, all right. But they could get fields pretty quick that way, between the trees even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was dry ridge like that too, where you grew up?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, it was not enough timber there. My dad didn't have six acres of field on him, I think, for a long, long, long time. So he had not decided all we got was enough feed for the horses and the cows. What we sold didn't amount to anything for a long time. Maybe sell for $100 a year. Big sale that would be in those days, you see. The wheat was only worth about 60 cents a bushel. You get $1 .20 for a sack then. It's different now, you know. So in 1920, when you were threshing, each farm family, farmer would only have maybe 50, 60 acres. Yeah, lots of them didn't have any more. A lot of them. Some of them were only 10, 15 acres. Most of them were up on this end, the little bear, they called it. You see what that was, me. He lived under the forger there, you know. We didn't charge him anything. We just trashed it for him free. He was hard up all the time. We only had about 10 acres of field. So we never did charge him anything for his job at all. We donated that to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hey, will you tell me who was on the crew? I mean, how big was your crew and what did the different men do?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We had six bundle pitchers and two sack sores. Sometimes we had three, that would be nine. And then we had a man at the hall of water, that's ten. And then an engineer, that's eleven. And a separated man, that's twelve. About twelve men. Crews, that's all we had. But my brother, he took care of the separator and I took care of the aignians. We took the big jobs that they paid more for. And the separator tender, he got about the same as the engineer did. So we had those two jobs ourselves and that's why we could make it fairly good, you know. Nothing big.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be how much you'd make when you were done dividing up the money? That would be you'd have four or five dollars a day for yourself?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, we never figured that. We figured what was left and then we cut it in two. So we took half and half. My brother, except in 19, well that was a big year we had, you know, I told you. We made money then. We made seven thousand dollars that year. So we got thirty or five hundred apiece. But that was more we made in three or four seasons before. But the war had been on and things were up, you know. We paid seven dollars a day for pitchers too then. See, seven dollars a day for sack sores.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you pay in 19, what did you pay before that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, two and a half for pitchers and the sack sores got three and a half I believe. Two of them, maybe seven dollars a day there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was a real shortage of labor after the war then?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, it was no shortage of labor. Well, there wasn't any big jobs you have, you know. Just when I was young I would work on a sawmill, get in thirty days maybe. And then we'd go out in the hay. When I went out in the hay that was the hard work for me. Then they got in maybe ten days. And then when the harvest come we'd get in thirty days about. And that was all the work there was. See, see how mills did. They were all over the country, but they were small and the only way we could get any logs in was in the winter. You couldn't log in the summer, you couldn't haul them on the wagons, couldn't get no load on, you know. So in the good winter they get more logs. In the poor winter they wouldn't have the big run. So it hardly lasted over thirty days on the sawmills. And you were through there. So a job, all around job was something we didn't know anything about hardly.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And the harvest was the best pay and was the easiest to get people there, you know. And it's as that book, you know, they come from way back in Kansas here. Because they crop earlier there so they got another thirty days in, you know, and they made pretty good that way. In those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your crew, were they local men or?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, they were all local. You know. See, there was quite a few people here that cost 160 acres or so. Most of them were homesteaders, you see, and that's all you could take. You could take 320 if you paid so much an acre. But very few people had any money to do that. They call that the preemption. Like Phil Deal's place, he took preemption and homesteading both. So he was the big farmer up there. He had 320 acres, you see. But he didn't have too awful much feel anyway because of timber all over, you see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: In order to get a hold of about 150 dollars then that's just as hard as we get a thousand now. It's easier to get a thousand now than it was 150 then. That's what it took on a preemption, I believe. So much an acre anyway.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So my dad, he filed a preemption to start with, you know. Then you could live so long and then you had to pay up or lose it. So he shipped it over to homestead then instead, see. That's the way he got done it. He didn't have that much money. There was no preemption to take around there. Anyway then they took up a prac level of the land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this on his original home place? Was it preemption to start with?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I got the paper here yet on that. I showed you the preemption and the deed. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have trouble with the machinery, the threshing machinery? Did it break down at all?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Did you have to... No, we didn't have much trouble. We had the fire the first year. We burnt out the first year and that was tough for us, you know. Smut fire. Burnt everything inside the separator and the blower fell off. It was on the hoof's place and that's the last place a little bear weighed on. So we thought we'd have a fix but we were gone in two days though after that. We called up the case people in Spokane and they shipped it down on a night train where the train would come here at eleven o 'clock at night. And then we had to put in all that stuff, you see. So in two days we was gone again.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And they thought the IWW set fire on us, you know. They told me about that. It wasn't them at all. They didn't do it. No, it was nothing but smut. We had the explosion day before, you know, and it was awful smutty green too but it was the black smut. So we didn't think about the gardeners have at all out on this place because you would see the big head here and there in the bundle. So there was that brown smutty and that was just right. Got the right mixture, you know. And what started it was electricity. You could go around the machines certain days and stick your finger up to a belt and you could see the sparks fly, you know. And that's what set it off there, I think. Electricity. And then something went through the cylinders and made a spark, you know, and set it off too. Rock or something or a piece of iron happened to go through the machine.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And it poof, was fire all over it at once.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No,

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I liked that job, trashing. I thought that was pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As an engineer, what did you do? What was taking care of the engine? What did you do to take care of it as an engineer? What was your job?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: My job was to run the engine and keep steam in the boiler, poking wood in it all the time. The farmer furnished the wood, you know, all that wood for you. And you steam it up with that, you know, and then get up in the morning about four, so you'd have steam by seven. See, at night it always had to start over in the morning again. So we put it in long days, all right. As an engineer, he put in the longest day, from four to about eight, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the separator tender? What did he do?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he took care of the machine. It was always something, you know, to fix. Not all the time, but, you know, something to shake loose or belt, break, or release it and have all that really and check up your stuff, you know. So he worked overtime quite a bit too. So that's what counts, you know, if you have the machinery in good shape so you could run the study without having any breakdowns. And how to set your machine, I told you about that too, I guess. One of the Nielsen boys, I don't know what's building up, but one of them boys, he went over on Big Bear, you know, and worked. And they had a name Fairfield that run the machine there, and he smoked a pipe and something happened to the machine, oh, golly, they're not. He feed it for the rest of the back one, feed this pipe up, and then he went down and fixed it after work. And then it took him so long to set the machine, you know. So Nielsen said to them, now them Nielsen boys, I want Little Bear to take them five minutes to set the machine, and we said, oh, little bear, you're a real bragger. We made fun of him, you know, and he told me this. So we had to quit Little Bear because the small machines start coming in, you know, and combines. So we thought we'd try Big Bear for a while, so we got the job down there. And I knew all about it, so I told the crew we had a nice level spot down on the flat there in the farm, so you can set down there on the flat on that level. So I told them, be ready now, we're going to show those people on Big Bear how long it takes to set a machine. It was level, so we didn't have to dig for nothing, you know. You had to dig for the wheels to get the machine level, you know, and same with the engine, it was rough ground. It was level at the floor. So I told them, they wanted to pull the belt out, they said, get it going when you pull it out, run out with it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know what all it took us?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Three minutes. You hear the button, we were sitting up above about 500 feet away from the machine looking at us when we were setting, you know. And I never stopped the engine, I just unhooked it, turned it around, lined it up, and they were there and throw the belt on, I backed up and threw it off the clutch and I never stopped it. And the fella sat there and I had to wave at him to come on down, come on down.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Laughter.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, yeah. Funny. Yeah, my oldest brother Albert, you know, he was a real mechanic and he worked with us the first two years and helped us out, you know. Of course, he had been with the head of the job like that before, you know. So he was fast too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That it was just a little bit off, you know. See, it had to be level so the weight wouldn't flow to one side and this and that. And they put a jack under there and told them, go ahead and pitch in.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, you know. And they used to talk about, my brother didn't say, I've never seen a fella like that. He can fix a machine with a plier when the others are looking for the tools. Laughter. And that was the truth or a past, you know. Then he seemed to know everything he did, one fella had to put the machine in, sit on the sack pile and first thing you see him jump off and start running down there to fix something.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: How did he know what he had?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I didn't know there was anything wrong but the sound on the machine didn't sound right to him, you know, something was choking up or something. So he'd be right there so he wouldn't have to shut her down even. See the blower would choke up sometimes, the straw in there, you know, and all that. And if you're there in time you can poke in there a little bit and you don't get the whole machine choked up, you know. The blower would choke up, there would be straw all the way through, you know. And that's the way he was, you know, he taught us all that stuff. That's why we had good luck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you learned it from Albert?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah. You know, I've heard it said that they used to say about you and your crew there that if anybody could thrash it, you guys could do it. If it could be thrashed, you'd be the ones to do it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's the way it should be, everything, but if they run the country like we're on the thrashing machine, there wouldn't be no depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl, how did you know, how can you tell when you could thrash it, when it was just dry enough or ready?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, we'd go out and test it, you know, we'd walk out in the field and then we'd stick your hand in the bundle, in the middle of the bundle, and pull out the head and see if it was tough or not, you see. If it wasn't tough, it was ready to thrash. And the rain, that'd be a little different, but that'd be the outside heads only that got wet, you see. Let me just test them, see. See, if it got a little damp, you couldn't knock the sheaf off the wheat, you know, and then they'd go out in the straw pile. You had to know that too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You didn't take much rain, you know, when chalking, when you'd trash chalks. You used a good shower, it would lay you off half a day. It was a real rain lay you off a whole week even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that happen to you very often?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, not very often, because, you see, we'd done most of that trashing in August and we'd let the rain come in August. After that, it was all stacked, you see, and they were all right then, see. So we didn't lose much time. Once in a while though, we had to lay off, I think it was only one harvest that we put in that we didn't have to lay off the rain. And there else you have to lay off two or three days maybe or so on, if it was kind of a rainy weather, especially when it was shocks. But if it was stacked, then you didn't have to wait too much. If it was a good rain, then you had to wait a couple of days maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what they were growing then, was it mostly wheat that you thrashed?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Wheat and oats, frankly, all of it. Hardly any barley at all. We didn't raise any barley up there hardly at all. And you see, they didn't have the machinery to farm like they do now, you know. We got some off of Lake Grameen, you know. If you could get a spring that was wet, you know, they only had horses to plow with and you couldn't get it in time and it would be late. Awful late.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: One place, one little bear, a feller named Harris, was farming it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And we come up there, you know, we're about through on the ridge too, and you know what he was doing?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was cutting the grain and we were trashing it. Yeah, it was late.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was an awful poor farmer anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did the grain usually sit after it was cut between then and when it was thrashed? How long did it take for it to get aged and be ready? When it was wet, you mean?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, no, when they cut it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would it take after they cut it to season?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It would take about a week sometimes before it was good enough. See, they cut them when it was pretty tough. You couldn't wait until it was ripe because then the binder would knock the grain out for you. See, you couldn't cut grain. It had to be tough yet. It took about a week out in the chalks and sometimes they even had to turn the chalks to get the inside dry, set the bundle this way if it was to come with them to fall. But they had the wheat here they called the 40 fold and we trashed quite a bit of it. That was to damn right. If you wait until it got real ripe before you cut it, you wouldn't have more than half of it. You know, that kicker in the binder, he just kept taking that straw all the time and he would distract it right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of this wheat?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: 40 fold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 40 fold?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yep. Just a damn long straw in the damn stuff. That tall. But it was good wheat and ripened early. That's why they put it in so much, you know. It ripened a whole lot earlier than the club wheat did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the wheat fall wheat or was most of the winter wheat or spring planted wheat?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, you could probably seed any wheat for fall if you want to. They didn't have no special wheat for that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The reason for planting in the fall is to get it ripe early, you know. If you put in all spring wheat it would never ripen up hardly in some years. It would be stand green, you know. Especially like the club wheat. They would put in late all that club wheat. And I think the 40 fold had to be seeded in the fall. It was really fall wheat. It was a bread wheat, you know. The reason they called it 40 fold was because it created 40 times as much as the seed. But they don't raise that anymore at all. We held so much straw. Now the wheat they got now, I'd like to trash that. Jesus Christ. We'd trash thousands of 1500 sacks every day. About this tall and hems that long on it. No straw, you know. I was out here one time and looked at this wheat.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Gee, you'd expect to get a crop from this?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yes, I'd get about 40 bushels. It looked like 10 to me according to the whole wheat, see. It was that thin we'd get about 10 bushels a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the yield in those days when you were trashing?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, we got a few trashes. A field that made 40 bushels, they'd talk about it all the time. 20 bushels was good wheat. Now it gets as high as 100. So you see the difference on the wheat too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the most you ever did in a day, do you know? Did you ever have any big record days?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's the one big picture we had there. We had 1142 sacks in 9 hour time. That's the biggest record we had. You never had any big job at a time. You had to mow so much. You lost so much time mowing from one field to the other one. There was no big job, but this fella had 100 acres of oats. I knew that the trashing was so unfinished, so I told him to set the machine in the center of the field. We were going to trash it all in one pile, I said. Then I went down to get the photographer. I went down and got him. We finished it in 9 hours, all right. There was 1142 sacks and that was about 4 ,000 bushels in that setting. It was on Big Bear. The case got a hold of that picture we had. We had a case machine, you know, and they showed one of that picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do with it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They took it back east and put it in the...

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They had to make a scene out of it. They had to call the trashing men. So the picture got in that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I got a few letters from back east. I read it, you know. One fella wrote... I told him how many teeth were out in one season. I got a letter from one guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, how can that be without teeth like that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He couldn't understand. So he was no trashing man. No, that. Teeth was the main thing in the trashing machine. If the teeth got too dull, it would chop the straw up too. The bundle wouldn't go in fast enough. So you'd break it up and get a lot of chaff in the sacks. You couldn't blow it all off. You did. You'd blow wheat too. So they had a little curve on the end like that. Well, if that wore off, then it wouldn't take it, you see. And the bundles wouldn't go in to the cylinder and they could raise hell with your feeder, break the chain or something because they wouldn't go in fast enough, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you sharpen it? Sharpen them up?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No. Someone had to turn them around, but then the hook was out of the way and it jerked the tooth too fast so it didn't knock it out. That's what I wrote and told this guy. But I said there's one way you can reuse them longer without taking them out. That's to bend them forwards. So we had a rig like that, the bar about that long, we'd put it over teeth and then bend the teeth forward and you'd get the right curve. But if it wore so much so it got a, then you had to throw them away, you see. And you couldn't turn them around because then the straw went in too fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did one of the chains last, the teeth?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, that depends on the grain a whole lot. It was easy trashing. Last year the whole season, putting it in the new set and trashed for 30 days or something like that. Last year old season. But we had that bar with us all the time so if they wore down too fast, we'd bend them forwards a little bit, you see. We didn't have to take them out to get the curve right, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you bend them quite a few times before you'd throw them?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: About one time would be enough then to get down to some arrow. You had to have it wired this way too because your teeth in the grates, there were teeth there and these went between like this, see. Yeah, they went in between each other, yeah. And then if they were off on the sides, then the heads would go through so you couldn't run the teeth on the grates. They wore on the sides all the time. They got tennis and ice in there. And you had to take, put in new teeth there and it was sick. Six rows if you had bad trashing. There was two rows in each grate, see. And good trashing like the 44 we used one grate only. And you get into the little club, you had to have them all in. Six rows to knock it out. And then it took a lot of power, really. Steam too, you know, because it pulled hard, you know. But 44, the wheat would fall out itself. So it was easy to trash but then it had some damn long straw, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the oats and beans? Were they very different from the wheat to thrash?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, oats was no different at all. Oats was easy to trash. You had no shadows there, you see. They hung on to knock them off and all. But beans, you had to be an expert to trash them. You'd crack them all to pieces. You had to have slow motion on your cylinders and it was double cylinder in machine, one back of the other one. So it went through two cylinders, see. But they trashed with one cylinder on but they cut the speed down but then they didn't knock them all out. Then you had to trash dirt. They wore the machine out so damn fast, you know, because they got dirt on the roots, you know. And it was just dust around the machine and they just wore out everything. They didn't last no time with all the bean trash here. And then we had two rubber rollers so all the beans had to go through the rollers to bust the dirt chunks, see. They were just as heavy as the beans so they'd go in the sack. So it went through them rollers and busted the dirt so you didn't get it in the sack. Or else you'd get about 25 % dirt in there, chunks like the end of your finger, you know. No more of that bean trashing. I didn't like that very much. It was too dirty. And then it was hard to pitch it, you know. There was no bundles there, you know. You had to tear them loose in the sack, you know, and dust. Everything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So thrashing beans was a lot dirtier than the others, eh?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's funny. It makes sense. Yeah. See, when they harvested them they had to cut the rig, you know, and they cut the roots. You couldn't cut them on the top, you know, then you couldn't get them together and fall to pieces. And then if they were ripe, you know, and you get rough, some parts ripen faster than the others, you know. Then they had to let them lay a long time in the field even to let them dry out, you see, before you could stack it. And they come in late, you know. It was hard to save. They lost bean crops every once in a while that way, you know. They didn't get them in time. But they got wise. They waited too long before they planted them, you know. If you plant them real early when the ground was warm they'd grow up pretty fast. But if you waited too long, you know, what they froze to, you see, you know, they were afraid of an early frost, see. So they planted them pretty late. But it was pretty good money and beans then. The way they farmed was a little bare and they made good because, you know, the summer followed. You don't do that anymore now, you know. They leave a field out, you know, and put nothing on it. That's what they call summer follow. And then when the bean business come in, they put beans on the summer follow and then they'd plant fall wheat on the same ground, see. So they made good, you see. You got two crops that way, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But they don't raise any more beans at all around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, the prices didn't come up fast enough, I guess. The prices were good then, you know, they made pretty good on beans. But they start irrigating down south and all over the raised beans, see, and they could do it cheaper and better. So they quit altogether.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well now, Carl, when you're talking about enjoying it, having a good time in the harvest crew, you say it's because you got a chance to spend time with people and get to know them better?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's what you did. It was something new every day, you know, especially for us that had a small farmer. We could ask for three, four farmers in a day sometimes. Small jobs, you know, and all that. Guinness evil, they had a different set up there. They had the cook wagons and there was big farmers there, you know. So that was a whole lot more. You had to have cooks and rust about. And you had to have one bundle team, so we had two. We had a heck of a crew to pay, you know. We had about 25 ,000 men when we had 12.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how you got to know a lot of the people around the country?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, I knew from everybody east of town here, you know, cleaning on Big Bear and Beery and Dry Creek and Noron, Little Bear, all of them.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were going around and thrashing for different people, were most people in about the same shape? I mean, as far as how well off they were?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: My farmer had, say, up to 100 acres of field or 8 acres, you know, they were pretty well fixed, you know. But there wasn't too many of them. But the small farmers, they didn't make much. But they worked out a whole lot of them people too, you know. They worked in the woods, you know, when they started the wood business up here. They worked out too on them small farms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that many of the families were just barely making it, or were most families getting by so that they were okay?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, they just made a living. It was not to buy much those days, you know. Like our family, we didn't buy much stuff. We raised all our food. And there was a little game in the country. We shot a lot of grouse for tea to eat and fish in the back, and stuff like that. And salad butter, eggs, trade for groceries. So we used very little cash, very little. If the average farmer had around $100 income, he was sitting pretty good. Yeah. Most of the women made the clothes for the kids. They had sewing machines. And they'd buy the cloth, you know, buy the overalls, make it. And one kid growed up, and the other one would wear it, you see, and so on. So they wore out everything. It was nothing thrown away. To find a tin can when I was a kid was a hell of a job. Of course, we wanted them to play with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The story about the rooster canning the man?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, that was pretty good. That's the only man I ever can. The rooster had to do it for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you tell me what happened there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he was kind of a queer doctor, this guy. So it wasn't a bean fetching. We took one man with us extra in case we were short -handed farmers. They furnished the pitchers and everything. All we furnished was the sex, the engineer, and about four or five men when we went out tracking beans. So we thought we'd better hire a man with us, you know, to have in case they're short -handed, then he can get up and help them. So we hired this guy for that, and I said, you don't maybe have to work at all, because if they got a full crew, you don't have to do anything. But if they're short -handed, then you've got to get out and help them. So we got over there on the beach, you know, and the sleeping in the barn, you know. We'd been out, I guess they'd been out about a week maybe. And he started cussing and swearing up there about four o 'clock in the morning. He was rolling his bed rolled up, and we couldn't figure out what the hell was going on. So he went, you know, and I asked the fella to slap close by to him.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I said, what in the hell is the matter with Charlie?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Quickly anyway, I said, that rooster corroded all morning. He got mad about that. So he left.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what's that story about the hooked nose, hooked noses? Would you tell me that one again?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's the Steelsons around here. They all got such big noses, you know. And I seen them the first time they were around. They lived on that bed, and the old men and the kids were about 10, 12, 15 years old, I guess. And somebody in the crew said, that's the best cherry pickers I ever seen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And no matter what you mean by that, don't you see that nose?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They hooked that on the limb and they picked on both heads.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You don't have to tell this, I would have it hit the name of Steemonsons. We're living here now, you know, three of them in town here. When the Steemonsons come and ask me about that one, they hit. And he laughed about it. Charlotte Spencer was the one that wanted the Spencer's marriage in the Steemonsons.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He was kind of funny, don't go right that way. You know, you can, other people, you can never tell when he quits. If something hits him they don't like, he just quits the oven and walks off. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference among the men that worked for you? Did some of them work a lot better than others? Were some of them a lot more skilled?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You always find that. And it's a little tricky, that too. I didn't have to hire too much men, but I found out a lot about people. You put them on the right job. He's good. And you put him on another job, he's just worthless. It's that much different from people, but people don't understand that. But that's why they have so much trouble. Some people seem to be worthless. You put them on the right spot, maybe he's real good, you see. And I found that out when I worked on the farm middle down there. I was kind of a half a boss there. I was middle right, you know. I straightened him up and one feller, he was sort of a funny guy. He kind of had less, you know. And Meade said to me, well I guess we'll lay off that feller that they bought the feller sawmill out and they're going to start new, you know, new people. So I guess we'll weed our crew out and we'll let that feller go over there, he said, and this and that. So they started up and they had the pail sawyer, is what they called them. They stand behind us all and take some lumber away, you know. And that's what this feller done. And, oh my God, they had two, three guys there, no one knew how to do it, you know. Good workers too. I said, you better go back and get Danderson. I said, he used to be here, he's an expert at that. And they went and hired him back. He knew just what to do, you know. See, it's a trick there, you know. If you take the slab away and let the slab fall the wrong way, you can't push it down on the rollers because you got limbs on it too, maybe in it around, you know. But you turn the flat side down and then the boards when you take them, you got to pull them this way and get the narrow side up so the edger men know how big a piece of board they can get out of that thing, you know. And that man was an expert there. But you put him in some other place, he didn't seem to know how to work at all.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that's the difference on people.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So if you line up the crew right, you know, you couldn't get along with anybody. And that's absolutely no nothing, but you don't find many of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell me about what the setup was in those little sawmills?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Take the one that you were mill riding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men would work in the mill and what was their different jobs?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, well I was mill riding down there in Kendrick, it was a small mill, they had about 12 or 13 men on it. One was a ratchet setter, they called, he pulled up the log, you know, if you wanted one inch board or two inch board, you know, he pulled the lever and pushed the log up. And then the sawyer, he was the guy that had done the sawing, running the lever, and then they had a turn down man, he turned the log with a kentook on the carriage, you know, turned the log over, and they got so far in, and then they turned three or four times, you know, and then they had the slip tender, but we never called them that. The pond duck, we called them here, that's what I done when I was a young kid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that job?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It was a long day, you know, you had to pull the logs out of the pond, see, up on the deck. Then you had the tail, then you had the trimmer man, he trimmed the lumber off on the ends, cut off the barky end and so forth. And the edger man, he's the guy that put the lumber through the edger and took the edgings off, you know, like you make an eight inch board or a ten inch board, you move the saws back and forth, you know, see. Then you had the man behind there, picked edgings, throw them away, you know, when they come through the edger. Then you had the lumberpilers to pile the lumber out in the yard and so forth. Then you had the engineer that run the Indians, and that's about all of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use to pull the lumber out of the pond with?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, they had a cable that went out, you lay the cable across the chute, the chute was like this, see, and the water was up here in the log, and then you pull them up on the chute with a pipe pole. Then you wrap the cable around them on the main cable, and then you had the drum out there and the friction, you went and pulled the lever and pulled the logs up, see, you pulled up three or four or five, depends on the size. Sometimes you only pull one log of big ones. I used to do that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that was the pond, what they call it, the slip tender, and that's where I got when I went on the coast that time here, told you about when I… What time was that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: 1917, I took $160 and said, I want to see the country now, I don't care if I get a job or not. Because I was going to be drafted next year, I knew the war was on, you know, so I went to Portland and I looked through the window and there was a sign there that said, single jackhammer, $4 a day, 8 hours. I looked at it and I said, I wonder when you take that job, that's what I've been doing with mining anyway, that's where the single jackhammer is when you hold it to bet yourself and then hammer it with the other hand. So I said, well, I guess, I don't think I want that job, I said to myself. Outside, working too much, and it's in the fall of the year, so I went a little farther up the street and there was a sign in the window, a slip tender, $4 a day too. So I thought I'd better go in and find out what that fella does anyway. I had said, what is the slip tender, he told me, and that's what I've been doing on the small mills here when I was a kid. So I took the job then.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I went to, among Washington, they had a shingling mill there.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And if I would come up, I told him that I wanted to be a slip tender, he looked at me, you a slip tender?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said, a small guy like you, I don't think you make it. He said, I had a man here that weighed 200 pounds, he quit. But he said, we'll let you try it anyway. For me, that was a different way of pulling out timber, it was easier because the man that saw the log off, they saw it off in 16 inches, the length of a shingling, you know. So you pull the log 16, well I don't want to hook it up. And one day I wanted to pull up four logs, man, four logs, they were the big deseeders, that was enough for two days. So I worked there about a month or so after both, oh God, it's getting to them right down here. I don't want to move here, it rains all the time, you know. No, he said, you can't quit, I got a job for you, go up and sit above the boiler, he said, and drop some extra fuel down, you won't get enough sawdust to heat the boiler, so you got to throw out slabs for them. I sat in the chair like this and the elevator went right by you and all I'd do is touch them and they'd fall down to the firemen, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So I sat there, I think it was only two days, three or four of them said, no, I don't want to sit there, I go crazy, I said, sitting there all alone doing nothing?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, you can't quit, he said, you go out and help the carpenters, you were building the mill bigger, so I stayed there for about three, four months. He said, so you can see, and therefore I found out about they had two names on it, the people there, and I come there and he said, hello there, master, do more, see less. I said, my name ain't that, well, he said, we're going to use two names out here, you know. He said, when you leave now, your name will see more and do less, but now when you're working, you'll see them do more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that country, that country west of the, on the coast?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, I didn't like it, I was on the, what do you call that, the wettest place on the coast anyway, Grace Harbor, little town named Elma there. The Saginaw Timber Company was a big outfit, you know, and they put the, they put the shingling in the mill.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: One all the time, so it kept you fairly busy when you had to pull up them slivers all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that country as compared to this country?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, I wouldn't want to live there, it's too wet. Funny part of it, the sun shone only two days when I was there, and then they stayed in and played poker. The crew, that's no fooling, not the male crew, but the ones that worked in the woods. And that was just opposite than it was here. When it rained, we stayed in and played poker. We wouldn't work in the rain at all in these summers out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's funny, why would they do that on a sunny day?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I don't know, it was too nice to work this head or something. That's the funniest thing that kind of tickled me too because it was just opposite here. See, we got a free board and everything, you know, in the zombies, so we didn't care if we had to lay off a day. We never worked in the rain hard. A lot of us working outside, you know, getting wet all over. We didn't have no clothes or rain. We stayed in. Then when the sun started, we stayed in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think on your travels? Did you see much country over there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, I didn't travel too much. I stayed there and then I went to Spokane. We went to automobile school for a little while. I learned how to run a car or fiction mostly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn much at that school?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, I learned in those days. We could take care of our one car, you know. In those days, nobody knew anything about a car, you know, so you probably had to do it yourself. Just coming out, you know. After that, they teach you guys. He's telling us how a car works, you know, and all this and everything. Then he said, if everything is alright, then the car won't start.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: What's wrong with it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Nobody got right.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I said, you know what was wrong with it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: A man on the crank, he said. She didn't have no self -starters then.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those people on the coast, did they seem a lot like the people out here?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, they made much difference. They're about the same. You can't tell one from another. There's a lot of people that lived here and went through the coast too, you know, and stayed there. And there's not too many that come this way from the coast. I don't think. But a lot of these early people, they went through the coast, you know, because they started to boom there, you know. A lot of them saw their homestead rights and went on the coast in the early days. There was one homesteader there close to us. He went to the coast and then a fellow named Ray at homesteader right close to where you are, he went to the coast too. But I think he had proved up all right, so he sold his places to somebody. Because it was starting to boom there then, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It was ending the cities.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, there's one more thing about the sawmills I was wondering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do those rollers do?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The rollers and the sawmills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The roller? Yeah, what do the rollers do?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Suppose the saw is over here now, see, and the logs are rolled on there and then it comes out here. Then you take the board, then you got rollers to push it down to the trimming and to the edumens and the slabs got to go down and the slabs were fused from the rollers. That's the ones that Ian Cartman put in. Yeah, you told me about that. Yeah, and the people looked and looked to see them. I'll never forget that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that you had to do to make them live rollers?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, you see there were one roller set in the bench, you know, with a pin in the end to swirl around. Well, the line shaft, you know, they had steam engines and then the big line shaft runs through the mill that they took the power to the edger, you see, and power to the trimmer. And that line shaft happened to run right under the bench. So we took all the belts and cut them this wide, two inches, and we put it on the shaft up to this roller, see, and then from this roller we had another one. And from that one this one. So we didn't have all the rolling. You see, there were rollers next to the, out there, there, that was about six, seven. We didn't have any of them. All you had to do was just push it a little bit and the live roller would take it, see. And that's the way we made it easier for the people.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And in those days they didn't saw on the timber that big, you know. They didn't see stuff like this all the time. So then when they squared the log on the carriage in those days, they made one by twelves, you know, they called it. That was the popular lumber then to build barns with, you know, twelve inches wide and they'd stick. Well, they squared that log up and saw all the one by twelves on the carriage. They didn't send that lumber through the edger at all, see, they turned the logs until they got it squared up, see. And two by twelves, and we saw them do the same thing and two by tens, they made that on the big saw. So the edger men didn't have to do that. All the edger men got was outside stuff for the log, you know, see, when you cut the bark off and then the next one be maybe four inches wide, and the next one eight and six. Finally they had the log squared up and then they saw it all on the big saw. And then you push maybe that many, stack a little bit up on them rollers. And that's where the log rollers come in, you see, he'd push them a little bit and they'd catch them and way down they'd go to the trimmer men. He'd trim them off on the ends, you see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know, I worked on seven different mills here and there's none of them left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would those mills work after they got the lumber?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it runs up to, like I said, about 30 days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's all? How much would they put out in that time?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, if they had a million feet, that was a big run, you know, but most of them had maybe 500 ,000 or less than that even. See, they do live. Because they couldn't mow the timber very hard and they had to have the mills for the timber work, you see, because you had no trucks and they haul it in on the slabs in the winter. Because you couldn't do much logging with a big wagon with wheels that high on and mud holes and it sank down and get stuck. Very, very few logs with the wagon and that was right close to the road already. That was tough going anyway, you know. You know, the wheel went out and the whole of the, it's a stick to team, you know, you couldn't get out even. But on the slab, you know, it was smooth, you know, it was like the ice is here. And they could take quite a load on the slabs in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they just cut the trees down in the winter or did they let them season?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, they cut them in the winter and snaked them down and then they rolled them on the slabs and hauled them in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Snaked them down with horse team? Huh? Snaked them down with horses?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, they would have eaten the couple of horses on the, on the slab too because they went up a draw, you see, mostly. They put a mill where it's a draw and then they'd log everything downhill, you know, see. So they didn't have no hills to pull, normally. Unless it was somebody like a farmer, he had cut a couple of thousand feet, he might haul it over there, you know. He'd take the time but he used to get something for it. But the real timber, they set the mills where there was a lot of timber, you see. So most of the timber came from right around the mills and it was owned by the, owned by the, owned by the owner of the mill. Yeah, the Etrals did buy some from the ones that had land there, you see. You'd buy from them so much a thousand and then they'd log it. See, you could buy logs for about fifty cents a thousand then up to a dollar and a half. Of course, the timber was all over and they wanted to get rid of it and they wanted to get field on their land, you see. And all that, so they, but like our home place, we never logged a log from that. No mill was one there, we could have logged two ones, but it comes so late that all our timber was down in the back Creek Canyon, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the mill have, was there species that you preferred in the mill that they wanted to have to log, to?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yellow pine, yellow pine and fir. Fir, they took time back too, but not so much. Yellow pine, they made one inch stuff from that and the fir was two inch stuff, two by fours and stuff like that. And then when the yellow pine got scattered, then they took more fir and some of them wouldn't log anything but pine, pine logs, that's all.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Cost us all the lumber around here, you know, pretty close, most of it too then. I heard the Troy Lumber Company was quite a big operation. Oh yeah, I worked for them too. We had the first mill up here, the white pine mill, that was a band mill, they put that in, that was the biggest one they had, or else they had a lot of small ones, three or four small ones around the country. Then they had a factory down here, they made sash and doors and all kinds of building materials here then too. And then they moved down to Lewiston and Clarkston. So they haven't got any flammings at the moorings, great ale lumber I guess. But they were putting big then, for those days I mean. See the Potlach Mill, they had come in in 1906 I think. Then it changed the lumber business quite a bit, you know, that big mill up there. And then the Elk River Mill come in 1912.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So then things started kind of booming around here, you know, there was a lot of work then, you go to Potlach, saw a mill and get a job, like I said there was no year round jobs, you know. So they had to start with, but then when those big mills come in they run year round, you see. So a lot of people went and got work there. So I imagine then the little mills started falling off about then too. Oh yeah, yeah, they were going pretty fast.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then when the Second World War broke on, that's how they happened to get a mill down in Kendrick and Yulee, you know, it was such a demand for lumber. That there was small mills, they bought stuff here and there and put them in.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know, there's a story I want to ask you to tell me about. You told me it was about a cow dying because his friend, the sheep. Yeah, I have never figured out about it yet. There's no home book about it, it's absolutely the truth. Everything that happened, I don't know. I don't know why the cow died unless you happened to get sick, but I don't believe so. I think it was because you killed the sheep. No one wants two animals to be together all the time, no matter where they were. You had five or six more cows, you know. And this sheep and the cow would be out in the field in the fall and they'd be together all the time, gather all the time. Then when fall come, we used to throw the hay out on the snow, you know, so we didn't have to feed them inside. And we done that. And we had the hay scattered here and there, you know, for the cows. And then this cow and her sheep, they were eating one pile and two other sheep come running from the other one down to that pile. And the old cow done and wrapped her and tore her whole stomach open right there with a butcher right there. And then when you got two eaten, we put them in the barn, like we always did. And my brother Oscar and I, we done the feeding and tied them up and we didn't have no stem cells, you know. And next morning, gone that old cow was standing up in the manger and trying to push herself out against the wooden log barn, you know. You couldn't push the wall down. And she stood there and she informed, you know, and grunted. You couldn't do nothing with her. She stood up in the manger and wanted to go forward, forward. And in about two or three days she come down and she laid dead in the barn down there. And that all happened from the time that she killed her friend. I don't know if they had got instincts like that or not. You mean, you mean that now the sheep came down and the sheep came down to get some, to get some of the hay and... Yeah, they had her two come. See, this cow and the sheep were eating from the one, and when them two come, the old cow didn't want them around, her sheep. And she hit the wrong one with her horn, you know, and ripped her open. See, that's what done it. My mother, he didn't know what to think about it. She, he thought maybe there was something in that alright too. Because there was nothing wrong with the cow. She was a milter all the time and feather in the barn. They called her a red sign because she had, she was a white cow with two red stripes coming down on her sides here, you know. But then I didn't talk to any animal doctor about that, so I don't know what they'd say. But she, they'd been friends for a long time before that. Oh yeah, a couple of years together like that. It wasn't parked at all. And that's something that now had been an animal with the same kind of fur or skin, but the sheep had wool and she had hair. And how they could be so friendly is the funny part of it. It never was a part, no time, no matter where they went. When down in the pasture in the woods, they'd be together there.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No more of that stuff that men could ever figure out, really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it come about that you had to go into World War I? When did, what happened there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: After that, I was that age, I was 103 years old then, and I tried to get extensions so we could finish up the harvest, you know. I don't know, I didn't get no extension, didn't get the farmer's extension all right, but it was just as important to trash the wheat as to raise it, I guess. So I didn't get any. I was out two days with the machine and I had to go. And then about 20 days after that, my brother was drafted. He was with me in the other one, in the trashing business. So then we had to turn it over to Albert. Albert and Bob, they had the trashes, they were three years old. Albert took over, no, Bob took over the machine from then on. And that was a good harvest too, you know. I had to get a poor treatment there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you not want to go at all anyway?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh no, I didn't want to go anyway, but you couldn't stop it, but they should have given us the same extension as the farmer's got to get the crops in. And them taking both of us too, that was the worst part of it. It was a big bean harvest that year too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We had the...

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I thought many beans said to us that year was the biggest crop they ever had. And we just kept our new machine with one year and we won $1 ,700 out of something, not $700 to get it. On the bean machine too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did it cost you when you bought it from Hawkinson?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: $2 ,500 for the machine there and then the bean machine, we bought that after that cost. $1 ,200 I believe, was a wooden machine, wasn't no good anyway. Felt pieces in two years.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, $2 ,500. The regular that cost about $3 ,500 new then. Steam engine, window horse, case engine, and a 32 inch machine. And water tank with it and wagon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say 32 inch, what do you mean?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Wide. And there was 32 inches this way through the cylinder. That's the way that we figured the machine was 28 inch or 24 inches in. 32 and made them up to 36 I believe the biggest.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that was pretty good business when you were doing it for you then. The thrashing was a pretty good business for you. That was an awful good business. Everybody wanted to be a thrasher man. They made a little money there in those days. It's about the only thing that wasn't money in, you know, except working for a dollar a day and that was nothing either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean it was better money than farming was?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yes. Yeah, if you had a good father, you know, you'd make pretty good. If you had something that paid you a thousand dollars a year, you know, that was a big income then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you sell?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: My other reason was all because the steam thrashing was going out. The combines was coming in and the gasoline power. So the farmers did buy a gas rig together, two or three of them, and thrashed their own grain, you see. I bought a gas rig afterwards but I only used that two years and I got rid of it. It was too slow. I didn't get enough for it because the next neighbor over here, he'd have a gas rig himself. And you had to go way off to get another job and it was scattered out. There was nothing there.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So I quit that and then I went into the automobile business down here but that was worse yet. Depression coming on. So I lived with two of them now. In 1930. So the tree depression, that was a bad one. Closed the pants up and everything else. I don't know what they'll do yet.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Maybe they closed up the whole country, huh? What do you think about it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Doesn't look too good to me. I think there's a good chance we're headed for a depression. Yeah, we got both at one time. We got inflation and depression. But the 1930s, then they had a depression only. No inflation. So you could do something. On the one hand, it would help you do something else, it would help the inflation again.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, the people are asleep. They don't know how to run nothing when it comes right down to it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Don't you know that every man that's walking is just as important as the other one?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They can't get that in a noodle, you know. They're going to pay one man $100 ,000 a year and another $1 ,000. If it wouldn't be for you and me and that, there would be no houses. There'd be nothing. Or it'd be important. Here these politicians, they get $60 ,000, $70 ,000 a year and they raise the salaries anyway.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, they can't run an agent like that. That'll never work. You see that everybody gets a job and that's where it should start. It should start with the people. I think we should have a 30 -hour wait now, myself. And it should be paid enough so you could live according to our country. And no overtime. That's another fake. Because then the people can't work and make a few dollars extra and no more because they don't want to pay that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And it's the same thing if you have a contract somewhere and you've got to move a whole lot of machinery in there. And you've got a job to finish before winter. Well, you can't pay them time and a half to finish a job so they have to quit and go back next spring. That puts the cost up. You have to restart, you know. And waiters are the ones that get hooked worse. They can't work over eight hours, you know, at the restaurant. Those eight hours are there and they may not have a goddamn person there. They should let them work 12 if they want to. But no overtime.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See? You think everybody could be given the same amount of money? Everybody could make the same?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I don't say it should be exactly the same. That wouldn't work either. But I mean it should have a pay according to how we live in this country. What it takes for a family of four or something.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Pay that kind of a wage, see?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then you should get away from this income tax business. Taking your money away from it before you can use it. That's the dirtiest law we have. When you go out and work here, you want to work so you can get some money so you can buy a car or a home or clothes or stuff. Then you take that money before you can use it. You should tax it at the factory. Manufacturing tax. That's where it should be. Then if you got a bad year and say, well, I'll wear these shoes twice as long now and I won't buy me no pants and that way you can get back up.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's adjusted itself. That tax system. The man who got a lot of money in, buys the same anyway. And I simply be to collect it. Just one man, one person you might say at the factory owns it. He does the business and he sends a check to the government and that's all there is to it. Here they got a income tax wakened. They dug for this, they dug for that, they dug for this. So it costs them 25 % to collect the taxes. Yes, it does. Under the manufacturing tax, it won't cost them anything. You know, you make so many yards of cloth and automobiles or whatever it is, but then the politicians come around and not the politicians but other people, they're queer, you know. They don't savvy up stuff work. You got to have labour, don't you, to make something. And you got to have taxes around the country. Those two things are with us forever.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But then they'll say, well if you make a manufacturing tax and the man that buys that material, you have to pay the tax.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well don't you pay for the labour? Isn't that worth the tax?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He don't kick a nut. So if you get something left in your store that you can't sell, you have paid for the labour. There you are. Same thing. It's here. You got to have the taxes to run the nation with. You got to have the labour to make the things.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And if they don't sell that part, they're out the labour, aren't they?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They're out the taxes too. Nothing wrong with it at all. But that's what they'll use, you know. They'll say, oh well now when I buy something I have to pay taxes on this and have it in the shelf.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Why don't they say I got to pay labour and have it on the shelf too?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Same thing. Because the taxes and the labour will not be there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carl, did you borrow money from the Bank of Troy when you went into business? Did you have to borrow? How did you get started?

speaker: Carl Olson
