words: Well I did too. I had the, I owed the bank $1800 I think when they closed it. But then I had that money on the account too and I asked them afterwards, I wouldn't have lost anything then. I said, you bet you would have lost. You wouldn't get paid for that money you had there. You had to pay the bill anyway. So you see I did all sorts of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you didn't have to use the bank? You didn't use the bank of Troy?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well I did for a while there. I had to, I wanted to sell the farm you know and then I'd get money in the bank of Troy. You don't have to sell the farm for that. You can come in there and borrow some money if you wanted. But I was just about ready to pay up there when I owed you know. I'd been buying a whole lot of cars and stuff. I think it was $1800 and I had that much on the checking account.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So, but I would have lost the whole business the way that all was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they good to you as far as giving you what you needed? The bank?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah. Oh yeah, if you get anything you know. The banker even, when I built the service station I had a son with me. We went to Moscow and didn't pay good there so I sold out. And we'd make no more. He ran behind the big hell up in Moscow.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So the banker, he come over to me and said, you built the service station?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said, no I can't afford it now. I said, I'm broke. No, he said, you go ahead and build. You go out to the lumber yard and get your stuff and charge it. He said, and build it. So I did. And the reason he done that was because his kid was the one that wrecked our business you know. But you don't have to say that to anybody. No, but this was only, only Bowman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you did a, first you had the, first you started in Moscow and then you came to Troy with the business?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well I first started here. I sold all from being here you know and things were going pretty good. So the Bowman kid, they come around and said, I want to go in with you. And much of it cost $5 ,000 or something. He messed that up. So he done that you know. Then the big outfit in spoke every day or two. They had a garage in Moscow they felt they wanted to quit so we bought them out. See up there. Then we moved across the street and took the Chevrolet afterwards. But then things started getting so bumpy you couldn't sell nothing. And the trade -ins were bad those days you know. Because all the cars you traded you couldn't hardly sell them because they were out of date, 100%. It's like the Model T now. And that Chevrolet first to come out you know they fell to pieces you know on 100 miles. And you couldn't sell that stuff. And you couldn't hardly make a deal without taking the trade -in you know. And the profit wasn't too much on the Chevrolet either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when was this?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: 1927 and 28 when I was up there. And then in 29 I went down here and built this service station.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that did the bank change much from from Bowman to Brockie or were they much the same in their way of philosophy, a way of dealing with people?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well they were both good that way. Bowman, he has a lot of Swedes here. He was a Swede in Lauterman. Come from the same part. So he got a lot of help from Bowman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The same part what of Sweden? Where?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, Väramland they call it. And right around there are students. And Brockie, he learned a lot from him you know. How the bank was run. Then they run this bank. It's like it belonged to everybody the way it should be run you know. If the man was good, well they gave him money and he didn't have to put up any mortgage or nothing you know. And that's what kicked Brockie out. They were running on the whole liberal way. And you got to do bank laws now you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And that's the trouble there. And there's so many people banking now too so you have to run the banks different you know. And I think that's the problem with such a thing as writing a bad check those days. I never heard of anything like that. Being in the red and all such things. But I heard that Brockie was real good at being able to decide if a person could be trusted or not. He was a real good judge of people. He did see. He was born and raised around here too and mingled with the people so you knew them.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He had everything back of him. And that's the way a lot of them got started here you know. They didn't have to put up any collateral at all.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It's like when I went down there and I stopped the moment I said I want to sell my place so I guess you don't need to do that. You can borrow the money here if you need any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you started selling cars Carl were they just pretty new in the country still?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well they weren't really new because they've been selling a lot of forts and Chevrolets and you know. In 1920s when the business, the car business started to boom you know. Then everybody started owning them in the 1920s. And I was down here in 1926 and 1927 and 1928 and 1929 you see so it was going up pretty fast. But then we got that depression in there you see and set back things. From here all the people that was dealing in automobiles and I went broke. Every one of them. Two or three in Lewiston and Spokane and here and down in Pullman. Because like I said you had to take in four trades and then you had to guarantee the paper. The banks wouldn't look at anything when you sold a car. They wouldn't take it at all.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then if you had to repossess it then you had to pay it up yourself. Even a new car you know. If you bought a new car you had to pay so much down then it was interest to pay and then it was insurance to pay and all that. So if you got a third down and put it on the paper, he hadn't paid anything that good. He still owed just as much as the car did. And then we get stuck with that you see what put us here. We could buy a new car for that money. The last money and the old. And they didn't have no they had one finance company that would take it and guarantee it but they raised for a higher that you couldn't sell a car if you used that. So you had to use that other one. And when you signed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why wouldn't the banks take it? Did you have many contracts fall through?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah we had just repossessed quite a bit. And that's what made it so tough you know. And then those old cars you know that you took in they were outmoded anyway. It was one car that the Chevrolet had they gave you $35 to smash it up. Take a sledge. Yeah because they didn't want to make parts for them anymore you see. And when you trade maybe you had to give $50 or $100 for that. But that was your profit right there see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So it was pretty tough flat they come down you know. And then they changed the models from 27 to 28. The 28 was twice the car this 27 month see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Twice the car?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Twice as good. Built. Everything in it. The 27 that fell to pieces all the time you know. So they come down and said we got four car loads of 27 cars you got to take you said. I said we won't take a god damn one. Well you said then you lose your franchise. I said what's the difference if we take the cars we're broke and you lose us anyway.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So we finally agreed then we had the trucks sold and there was a truck on one of them so we took one of the car loads because we had that truck on it. But then we had three 27s anyway there and we had some on the floor. And you will have made $90 profit on those damn things you know. And the new Chevrolet didn't cost any more they were worth $200 more at least. Just even the looks on it. They were a little bigger and nicer and better built all over. So you see how pickled that was. No.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: One went there and repossessed it. The client had a man come by and said we got to go down and repossess it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Model T on Texas Ridge. Better go with me. So we went and there stood way out on the rim rock. I said leave it there let's go home. It's going to cost us more to take it home than it's worth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they done? They throwing it over the side? How they got it down there?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I don't know. Somebody they couldn't pay for it. He didn't give it to them. I guess he drove it down there and left it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did I talk to a dealer from North Dakota after I was in the service station and how he came out?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh he said I went flambusted. He said they come around the finance company and said you got to repossess the cars. Well he said if I do I'm broke. No use. I said I can't do it. Well the finance man said we let it go for six weeks and then we'll go out and see if we can do it. When we went out it wasn't a god damn tire on the cars. They stole all the tires, broke the windshield and everything. Ruined them. He lost everything. I think it's easy.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It won't be much better now if it gets really bad. I think 90 % of the cars, 50 % of the cars, 0 % or maybe more than that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Who are they going to sell to?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Most families got two cars already or three. There's no opening for a used car. If they had to take them back, why you wish what a pickle that would be. They never come out. No, they couldn't sell them anywhere because they already got cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were selling cars, were most of the cars the first one that the person had owned?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: A lot of them. I had to train people how to drive too. Quite a few of them. But he said never train your wife to drive because that won't work. He said let somebody else do that. Because he said he gets so nervous and funny. The best place is to go out on a field somewhere where there's open ground. They learn the quickest there. Because if you get on the road, they get nervous seeing out a car coming. If you get on the field, they can drive anywhere. They can stop and start. So I done that a little bit. I took them out on the whole thing somewhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is this he you're talking about? Who is this man?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's one of the territory men. I come to check to see if everything is alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you took people out and taught them how to drive? Was that hard?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh no, it was pretty easy. Because it was pretty simple. When they jumped from the Model T to the gear shift, it was a little personal. But for the driving, it was alright. I guess I told you about that. A man come around and the fella told him that I traded cars up here two years ago and I got beat. He said I didn't want anybody to know about it. No, he totally shavily got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what's the matter with him?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, it makes a hell of a noise in there. He said when I was going to back up or get in the garage. So he said I'll go wait you home and see. So the car, two years old, brand new, he used to robot home that guy. There was nothing wrong with it. He was going to back out. He didn't push the clutch in. He pulled on the lever. And rather than the gears start pulling together, you know. And they asked him, this fella said, why don't you go back and tell him, oh I didn't know, I thought somebody had beat me. He said, and I don't want anybody to know that I get beat, he says. That was true, that story. It was the shavily man himself that told it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he left it sitting in his garage for two years?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, never moved it. He used to robot home. He traded off Model Ts. And he thought the outfit beat him. Showed him a rattle trap, see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I've heard stories about how when they first came in with the cars that people would try to stop them by yelling whoa. Yeah, they done that too. Yeah, they done that alright. But to take the Model T was pretty easy to learn to drive. If you could steer that's about all you had to do. You had your pedals there, you know. Pedal for back up, pedal for brake, pedal for second gear and all that. It was a pretty good little car. Because they had all four roads in them too, you know. But also you had to carry a stick with you to see if you could get across or not. One fellow went back east with a Model T and said he drove that car about 40 miles an hour all the way there, he said. On the highway, see. Never hardly ever broke down, you know. The Model Ts were tough. You had to do a lot of upkeep on them like the ignition system, you know. They had them coils in there, you had to file them so they cut the electricity off right, you know. That's about all you had to do in them. And then put brake linings in. So nothing else broke on them. Hardly ever. And they were having pretty rough too, you know. And these kind of roads you had, you know. Back up and they could run at it to make it over the hill and all that. But they had the best steel in them Model Ts was ever made, you know. Pretty good all of them. Swedish steel in all the Chefs and Exiles. They came there for Peterson and Carlson started their ranch factory from. They bought Model T axles and braces and that's what they made them good tools from. Even the fender brace, you know, that come out the whole lot of fenders, a little rod like your finger but it tapered kind of.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And you know what they made of them?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They just took them in there and cut them off and made plungers. Best plungers in the world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What factory is this?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's Model T Ford. Oh you said Peterson and. Yeah Peterson and Carson. They used to come around and sell tools from those in business. You know sockets and all kinds of things. And they started from that buying up old Model Ts racks and stuff and took all that good stuff. They axled us out in the braces and made tools from them. And they guaranteed their tools, you know. That's why they got such a good name. In order to beat that Swedish steel, you know. And you put that in the axles and stuff. Something they had to carry in the load. They always put the best steel in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well where was the money in the business for you? Did you get it from selling the cars?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Boy did I ever made any money. I never made any money there. I made more working. Trashing and working. No, and gas spaces. I didn't make no money there. I was right to the depression with the whole works. I just had the station finished in 1930. And it was depression after that. But I was single and I lived upstairs and had a bed up there. It didn't cost me anything to live. So I didn't lose it. But I got a good break when I got through building that station. And the shop back there too, you know. And the feller hauling oil to him from there, he bought it. And I got $8 ,500 for it. That's what I paid for the whole works. So I had the shop left. So I come out there, you see, or else I've been broke. And then at least a back problem, you know. So I run it then for about 8 -10 years. I didn't make any money though. I was sitting in there watching the better day. Three cars come in there and bought gas.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You know how many trips I had to make out when I was there to settle that much gas?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: 20 trips. Well, they bought two gallons, three gallons. Four gallons was a big sale. That was two bits a gallon, one dollar. And they were going to buy $6, $7, $8, $10 for a gas of three cars.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And there's ten times as many cars out now as there was then. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: We only got three cents a gallon profit.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: That's what you got then? Three cents a gallon?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Hell yeah, you go out there and sell dollars worth. Four gallons, you make 12 cents. And you had to wipe the windshield for them and put the chains on for nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Put the what on? You had to put chains on?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, we never charged for that. Because the other service agent didn't. So you had to do what they did. Now they charge you a dollar and a half and put the chains on. You get a lot of... it was no profit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about in repairs? Did you make money fixing up cars?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, we never done anything. At least the shop out back there. There was people doing that. I got $30 a month rent from it, you see, and that's what kept me going. See, I slept upstairs and went across the street and got a hamburger and a glass of milk and I slept upstairs.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Then I had $600 in the safe, I had a safe down there. I didn't deposit any money there for three or four months. I just put the cash in the safe and deposited the checks on it. So I had a little money in the depression.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It helped me collect some bills that way too. You know, you come in there and want to pay with a check, you see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And I got squared up and the banker said, where in hell did I try to get all the money from anyway?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I sold the bank in Troy, there's any good, all the women said.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, I didn't want to quit depositing all the other because that would hurt the bank. I knew that it was going under. A fellow from, a big boat from Spokane told me that and then I told his veteran dear, they bought my service station. I said, if you got any money in the dear bank, you better pull her out. No, no, he said, the big bank in Spokane is behind it, he said. Well, I said, that's why it's going under. He didn't take it out either. They lost quite a bit up there. No, he said, the reason they're going broke, he said, this fella told me, they got so much money loaned on timber. And you couldn't sell a board in the Depression, you know. The timber was over blessed. And if you had it on cattle, then you were finished right. You couldn't sell the cattle and you had to feed them and that cost money, you see. So that was poor loans, you see, and that's what this fella said. He was a government man, I think. And he said, they're going broke all them names, he said. But not the bank of Troy. The bank of Troy made it. Yep. They made it. There was only one around the country that made it. Two.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I wanted to ask you about prohibition, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. I was going to ask you about what you remember about that, those days. He told me there was once you just, you got arrested. Yep. But you didn't do anything. I took a guy home then.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I was working for, on the service station down there, that Dinsmore. He had the first one here. I worked for him. And he was open until 11 o 'clock, you know, when I closed the doors and he had his money in my pocket and everything. Then the fella said, I want to take you home. You take me home, he said, I was too far to walk. And I was the fella, I owe him some money. Trash bill, he wouldn't be a trash bill. I thought I'd be on the good side with him and took him home. Then I went to drive up to his house and said, no, it's a rough road. Stop here by the road. And we stopped there, here comes a policeman. And he had a pop bottle full of whiskey. And we sat that in the car and jumped out and said, I want to see who they are. And then I found out they were a police man and I was going to throw it out, you know, and they grabbed it before I got rid of it. So we got arrested for having liquor. But I didn't get anything for that. I threw it there, but they wouldn't take my word. I said, look here, I'm no bootlegger or anything. I said, I'll work a boot. I said, there's a station down here, there's a check sitting here and everything. The next morning, you heard it the next day and you come right up to Moscow and turn me loose. It's the whole story. But he got little firedman, little... 30 days or something, I heard about 20 days or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they believed you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: They believed you after... Well, I had the proof. I had nothing to do with it. I couldn't.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But I went up to the trial and he had a fish guy, you know, one of them. And I learned a whole lot there. The people on the way, the stem lies only. They never tell the truth. I didn't tell one thing the truth. Here's what he said about me.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He reached down here, he said, do you want a nightcap?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And handed it to me and that's what he did. He used to want to help here.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And everything they ask him for here, it was the same story. It was all lies.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about Sam McKean's moonshining?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, he was one of the experts here. He used to want to sit on the box and have the whole gathering under when they come out to search him. They never left the box up. There was whiskey sitting out on the lawn. The duty was common, I guess, so he took the whiskey under the box and sat down on it. He searched his house and he didn't find nothing.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Everybody made whiskey per mere dime. After he learned how to make it. It was foolish to pay $30 a gallon of moonshine when you could make it for 50 cents.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I would have did it in snow, but that's true. I'll tell you how it happened mostly. See, we didn't have no shoes those days. We only had the leather shoes. And you used them in the winter to get wet. And then they got so hard you couldn't get them on your feet. You had to grease them up when they dried out. So we had to run to the barn and back and fed the cattle barefoot. We didn't run out in the snow or anything like that. That's the way that happened. So we made our own shoe grease. We got a hold of a big cast iron kettle and then cut a whole lot of chips. It was pitchy. Then we built the fire outside and got the tar out. You know what that is. Then we mixed that with lard, tallow, boil it, and that's what the grease was used with. And we didn't have any grease then, so we couldn't get our shoes on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then you had to run the barefoot?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, we had to run out and feed the cattle. You could have put somebody else's shoes on, one or two, but we just done that for fun.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See how we like it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: About 150 feet, the barn, and back. So it gets pretty cold. We got kind of a kick out of it, you know, too. But we couldn't get out in the wind very much, but leather shoes only, you know. I don't know how your feet could stand it. Well, the one time we could stand it when the snow was real frozen hard, we could run on the top of it, then the leather shoes would do pretty well, you know. No, I mean in barefoot, how you could stand on the barefoot. Oh, well, we had to run fast, you know, and up to the corner. It was all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So everything you told me is true?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Everything. Everything. I could maybe tell you more too, but I don't know. Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You didn't make your second lady and show your wife, did you, that I showed you here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, Mr. Anderson, the clown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what was that again?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he played the jew's harp, this guy, and he went between the house, Holmes, and he lived there right close to it, and played for fun. He made a horn of bark, and you could hear him for a quarter mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Made a horn of bark?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, and sang in it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then he played this jew's harp, and then they always ask him, are you single?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, no, I've got my wife with me all the time. And then he'd take that out of his box and put them two eyes here, and little hair, and here she is. She's with me all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, no, did you see her? I showed you that here, didn't I? Were the two eyes little buttons?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: What he used here, regular doll eyes. You know, how they were almost a tip back and forth. That's what he got a hold of, and they fit good here. But I made me a pair here for fun.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Maybe I lost a tune here. He has a couple of beads. See, I put that there. Say hi, you see.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: There's the eyes.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Like this.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And you wrap your handkerchief around there, and you've got a real person there. You see there live too, it moves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's, Carl, what's the story that goes along with the Heaven and Hell? What's the story that goes along with that? With what?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The Heaven and Hell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: The Heaven and Hell. If I was to tell what that is now, I'd use it between Nixon and Agnew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story that he used to tell?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, he used to press events all the time. And this other, I told him that they were dead then, but Nixon and, if you want to make a good story about what I do, I'll say, well, here's Nixon and Agnew going to Heaven.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then Nixon says, what did you do with my passport?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, don't you remember, Agnew, you told me to sell that for $50 ,000. And we split the money.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then Agnew said, oh, don't worry, you can have half a mine, we'll get in anyway. That's the story I'm going to use on it. He said, do you get a half a mine, we'll get in anyway, and then he gives him the bad half. Yeah, he gives the bad half to him. And Peter said, you go down there, you don't belong up here. And when the money gets across, he says, come on in, come on in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what's the song that, what was the song that Victor Anderson used to sing?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He used to sing a sweet song. For you set the ring for the new love, Peter Hahn, O 'ing from the night that blooms on the horse, As Carl Luther Breen, me and my dum's a -lengin' my hair for yon.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Do you understand it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Come on, you know I don't.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: May I put the ring on your little white hand, and the crown in your hair, And you shall be my maiden so long as the hair on the world. That's the way the song goes in English. It's a nice song, you know. May I put the ring on your little white hand, and the crown in your hair, And you can be my maiden so long as the way you're here on this world. That's what he used to sing. With this ring and see. With the juice harp and the... Yeah, juice harp, he played that all the time. He was a really clown. He haven't got any clown as good on TV as he was. He's kind of funny looking too, you know, so he could put on. That's old Vic Anderson. His brother lives in Clarkson, he's 93 years old, he'll be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that Art Anderson?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: In victory he was the oldest in the family. Well, he had four or five girls, three or four girls too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He once had arrived by, so it wasn't too bad. He was home, the old man. But her brother, he lived there too, but he had to go to work like the rest of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years was it before he built the house?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, I don't know for sure. He built first a small cabin, I think, and then he added on to it. And there's all logs, you know, huge logs and dovetail together. Not the regular, you know what the dovetail looks like. I don't see how you could do all that and get it to fit like that. I told my brother to leave one of those buildings, but he tore them all down. So you couldn't see how the dovetail was. And then the house had to burn up on the top of it. And then after they got the houses built, then they went up with the big broad -ax and smoothened the walls down, see, so they got smoothened. Then they put lumber on the outside to make them look better. But the house I was born in was a long cabin. But when you see the picture of it, there's boards all over, but the logs are still in there. And then they had the scaffolding, you know, and that was quite the update. They called them broad -axes. They had a big broad -ax about that wide, you know. And then they'd have a scaffolding and then they'd go like this.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And smoothened the walls even all the way down, see. There was one man up here that called Johnson. He was an expert in that broad -axing. They hired him sometimes to come and do the broad -axing for them.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So they were pretty good on woodworks, the Swedes. They come from a wood country, you know, when Sweden was sort of...

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See, my dad, he was only 22 years old when he homesteaded here, you know, and how can you learn all that stuff that way?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I can't figure out. He was a carpenter and he laid bricks and all that, so that's helped him. It isn't very many houses around, I'm told. I don't know, a lot of them, but... On the dry creek ridge that really wasn't the lawman built. He was with pretty everyone. Come and got him all the time, you know, to do any carpenter work. And then when we made the brick out there, he'll have the bricks and the chimneys for them too. So he worked out quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you have his fire at the house?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that's when the hymn lived there alone. No, it wasn't alone. They were both homes. I don't know, it caught from the chimney. It saddled somebody, you know, and made open a crack like that in it, see. And the fire in the stove, I called upstairs, you know, and there was no chance to get up there either. It was way up in the attic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about for your mother, the kind of work that she had to do with all the kids?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, a lot of work. She done all the seeding. By hand, when they farm, you know. My dad, he run the heroin and heroin down. See, the seed was on the ground and then did heroin work to cover up the seed. So she done all that work. I used to be watching him have this bag here full of weed and she'd go like this. From the side to one hand, the other hand. Well, she was quite a worker. She was a pretty husky woman too. And then she milled cows and took care of the cows. All the time. All those kids in the family. You had so many brothers and sisters. Yeah, it was eight boys and four girls. They had quite a family.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But to start with, it would be the worst anyway, you know, because when they have the first kids, they're all small, you know. But after they all started growing up, then they got help. See, they'd help them, you know, but the first, until the kids got about eight, ten years old, you know, then they could start helping a little. But they had kids that were just born in one year or two old and I don't know about three or four and so on, you know. That makes a lot of work.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So that was quite a thing. But after they grew up, then they got help from the bigger ones.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So you see, this country isn't very old. It isn't more than a hundred years old about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you see how fast things have moved the last fifty years?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: This country is going to ruin itself if it doesn't change it. It's going to all the pieces. Well hell, damn it, they're taking all the big farmers, taking all the land now. Now they're trying to get them back on. I've seen in Canada, they're working that now. The government buys up land and he sells it to get the people back on the land.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Why are you getting driving?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Everybody's in a big city. There's crime there and fighting and stealing and everything else. When they made the law, the law was 160 acres is all you could take of land. Why don't they make a law now that you can have about 300 acres only and then split it up and get the people out and everything would help the whole nation. Because all these smaller farmers, they got two or three cows, they got some pigs, they got some chickens. And it wouldn't be a trust on all that stuff you're buying. They made butter and solids. See, now they have to come from a big outfit and they raise the price and just control the food practically. And now it would be worse than ever when they raised this five dollar wheat. That was a real land killer. Because if you and I sat here and wanted to buy 160 acres of land, in the five dollar week them big folks would bid a thousand dollars an acre. And we could never pay it off if we bought it because we didn't have enough. See, they do things so crazy here. I don't know what to think about this country anymore. There's no sense in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much of a feeling about when things started going haywire? Or who the president or when?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, I don't know what started to go. It was too free I guess. They didn't regulate the right things. That's what I got it figured out. The big guy took over everything practically. He took over the milk, he took over the farms, took over all the factories, took over all the small stores. They're going out of business all over because there's big chain stores and they control the prices.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So it's no good. And another thing, we make too much stuff that we don't need inside of the people. I talk to people down in the grocery store and they say, by God, they're getting sick. I can't even buy anything anymore because I don't know what to buy. There's so many things here I don't know what I want. Here you have a dozen kinds of cereals made from the same thing. Only one is rolled this way and the other one is rolled that way. That makes it hard on the dealers. They got to carry so much stock. It takes money for that, you see. Now when we went to the store when we were kids, about five, six articles is all we had to buy. We went home, bought them coffee and sugar. They had their own milk. Never had to buy any milk. Never bought any sugar. But they sold butter to those stores, the people that didn't have any farms. So they could buy the butter there, you see. And the eggs and stuff like that. The coffee did come in big barrels, you know. You'd scoop a couple of scoops in the packaging, wrap it up and soak it home. Put it in the old grinder and grind it yourself. You had better coffee too. That's why they sealed the cans now so tight. They lose the flavor when you break it up.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I still grind my own coffee. I grind my own coffee. Well that's what you should do. It's better flavoring. But there's one thing you can do that I didn't think about. If you got ground coffee, put it in the fridge of air. Then you open it once. See, it don't steam off then. When it's warm, it steams it off and it loses a lot of the flavor. I wanted to ask you about the family too. You said that they weren't religious. Your father, he wasn't religious. But your mother, she was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right? Was what?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: It was religious. Well, he wasn't very religious at all. He never went to church at all. But he was brought up in Sweden. I guess the religion had a big power over them. That's why they left the country for the bankers and the religion and militaries. You had to go and... In fact, you had to go to the army and spend four years there all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you said that your father and she, they used to disagree about the Bible?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah. Well, she didn't read much. My dad, he read all the time. He was posted and everything. He read all the time. And lots of them don't read. They just hear a few good things, but they read the whole thing out. You can find out yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what would they disagree about?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, something about, I don't know, marriage or something. And Paul said something and the mother was against it. And Paul said, you go and read it in your Bible yourself. She did. And then she told me I never taught anything like that was in the Bible.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So you see, here a few preachers tell you something. They just take the highest spots and talk on that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, the thing I was wondering about that, and I asked you this a little before, was that if you didn't get a religious... You know, they didn't teach you religion very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they, they taught you though to be good to other people?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah. That was the main thing. Honestly, if there was any crooks around our neighborhood, we could never associate with them. People wouldn't allow us to be close to them.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So when I went in the business, I got fooled. I didn't find out, I didn't know much that many crooks knew or heard of the most. They would pay us over four thousand dollars the time I was there on gas.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: So you see how much you lose then?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Because it was such a small profit on gas and four thousand is just putting it like taking it out of your pocket. But if you're selling something that you double your money on and lose four thousand, you don't lose so much. But on gas, what's such a small margin, you know, awful loss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did your parents teach you then, as far as dealing with other people, getting along with people?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, they just told us to be honest and do the right thing and help people if they need help and stuff like that. Everyday life that you live is simple. If you do those things, you have no problems.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the, you mentioned to me about getting a black spot on your heart?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh yeah, they told us that if we swear too much. Every time we swear, you get a black spot on the heart, Ma used to tell us. When that gets black all over, you die.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, that maybe was pretty good teaching for a kid so he wouldn't get too rough, huh?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I tell you what's wrong with the country too, you know, a lot of people, they were poor that come through, but it's old now that you don't die that look like. And all of us were reading about rich people, you know, how nice they have it. And they come here to get rich. You come here to get rich, that's on their mind and that's what they always seem to give a damn for. It's more than riches that counts, you know. But that's the ones that talk about that rich man, he got everything, he got everything and of course you can't blame him for that.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But I never got rich, I wanted to get rich alright, but I had a different idea in my head. If I got rich, I was going to help the poor. Because I know what it was to be poor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you really feel that way?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, that's why I invested a lot of money but never hit anything. I was in the oil fields in Montana, mining, mining, several places, bought stocks and a lot of different mines, never hit a darn thing. The only thing I hit the toll was the brickyard here, I had a talking death for that, I didn't have much. I doubled my money there, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how were you going to help the poor if you had money?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, if I seen someone that needed a better house, fix it up for them, not keep donating to them all the time, that don't help anything. Do something for them that does some good. Some people have an injection and only got kids and they can get nothing and you never get a hold of nothing. Some people you can't help too but that ain't a very big percentage. Put them on their feet so they can make it. See if you get behind one time and it gets so hard up that you have to pay, that's all their life, you'll never get anywhere. You're working for somebody else all the time. Oh yeah, there's one more thing I was thinking of too. That was when you quit school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what was the story in that? Why did you quit?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, there was many reasons. I didn't like the teacher very good and they didn't teach anything that don't mean good. I was there good in rep

speaker: Carl Olson
words: and I got some money so I could buy me some better clothes and stuff like that and that's what done it. But I didn't know I stood there and I looked at the schoolhouse and I looked at where we lived and finally I turned and went home. I came with everyone back again but they didn't teach you much, help you much in school in those days. You didn't have much schooling anyway. Three months a year I started with. Then I went to six, finally. Then I had a teacher and he got thirty dollars a month and then they raised it to sixty and I quit. The teacher had sixty dollars a month I believe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you come to blows with this teacher when you left? Did this teacher hit you or did you hit him?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, no, I never was hit. I never had any trouble with the teacher. It was something I said or something that he didn't like. I forgot. I was out playing with the other kids I believe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get married?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, I was good in school. I never started any trouble. Never stood in the corner. Never had to sit in afterwards like to punish them. You had to stay in school after it was over. None of that. Never.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I went back and seen one teacher and seen for a long time and she said, you know who I am?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No, I don't know. I said, I used to go to school for you.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Oh, you're Albert?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He said, no, I'm the younger one.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: How in the ... is going to ever forget you?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: You were my pet in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you quit school?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I was fourteen, nineteen -nine. Nineteen -nine is when I quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you quit then, did you go to work right away?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, not right away, but I got a job from my uncle then to be a clerk in a store in Helmer. It was a little town there. He had a little store there. So I was there in nineteen -nine and ten. Nineteen -nine and ten when Halley's Comet was on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You saw Halley's Comet then?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And the fellow that was my partner, he was a barber. He had one leg, one leg. And he done a little bootlegging. He sold beer, you know.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: See, they come out with two beers. One with no alcohol and then the real beer. You could get that yet, you know, but it was against the law to sell it in Idaho. So when they come in and bought beer, he gave them the good beer. Then when they went home, he gave them the near beer. And they come back with a dozen bottles of what you do with them. I throw them in the goddamn gutter, one fella said. Give them the good beer. That's what I want.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, what?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: And then they were searching down in Deary and I come home late. I went down with a couple of boys and we missed the trains and went to walk back. So I come home about midnight up to Helmer and I told them, we have a new man who's in Deary searching all the beer bars and everything. I said, you better get your beer hidden. So he had the barrel upstairs, you know, he had the roller down the stairway and the roller down the kicking house. But nobody ever come though.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Martin Lowe was the name of us. I had to fix on his wooden league every once in a while as soon as it didn't work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What went wrong with it?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: He lost it. He had the wooden league and the joints here, you know, would get wiggled too much. You had to tighten it up and screws come loose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you went down and warned him? You went down and warned him?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah. We slept upstairs in the same building that I was in. So I woke him up and pulled him back on those beer fellas. I was like, the shirt's used too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now was that your same uncle when they were building the railroad through, you pulled out some of the stakes?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, that's the one I stayed with on the meadow up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do that time?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Well, there was a nice meadow there, you know, what we looked for was bigger fields and everything in those days. It was timber all over and I didn't want it to ruin the meadow. So I pulled the stakes out and hit them. But they built the railroad anyway. They didn't pull out very many of them. I was only about nine years older, I guess.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I was down to the Severes when they were there, you know, and talked to them and everything, you know. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weren't there a lot of foreign workers, Italians, and people like that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, a lot of Italians here at that time. They built the Kramer Mill and that. 25 % of them, I believe, were Italians. They couldn't talk English at all. But most of them, they went back, you know, they come here to make a little money and then go back to get to it.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But I guess they were pretty fair to work. But they had to have a boss over about every four or five of them. They showed them how and stuff like that. Yeah, there was a lot of them in that river.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you tell me, Carl, that Johansen, the man that started up Nora, the mill there, you told me that he made some money by, he was sort of a tricky guy?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, he was a tricky guy. He pulled up the saw mill and took all the insurance money and the spotter didn't get any. Then he had a kid to carry it for money. Come over here so they wouldn't search him.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: But he was kind of smart too, you know, but he started a break yard up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there something about him using Confederate money? Did you tell me something about that?

speaker: Carl Olson
words: Yeah, Axel Bowman, he told him, you better pay up that guy now that you're Sweden back in, of course, they were neighbors back there, from Sweden. Yes, he said, I'll send him the money. He sent Confederate money too.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: No.

speaker: Carl Olson
words: I should really get going, Carl.
